next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Kai's left ankle clicked when he crouched into the starting blocks, the natural kind, a tendon snapping over bone. His right ankle adjusted a fraction later. Synthetic muscle tightened to set the joint before he'd finished settling into the pedals. He'd mostly stopped noticing the difference. Today it registered faintly, like spotting the same misspelled word on a sign he passed every morning.

Lane three. The indoor track had its own atmosphere: the rubber smell of the surface, the dry high-altitude air that sat in the back of his throat, the overhead lights throwing a flat white glare across the oval. The ceilings were high and packed with acoustic dampening, which took the sharp edge off footfalls without killing the echo the room always kept. Two weeks at Kirchberg had been enough for his lungs to stop complaining, but each morning's first deep breath still carried a small sting at the bottom of the inhale.

Six other sprinters were warming up. Novak on the far lane, doing strides with a gait so bilaterally perfect it looked unreal. Priya Chandrasekaran near the curve, shaking out her hamstrings, her right leg extending and retracting with the evenness of a fresh calibration. Two athletes Kai knew by face but not by name were jogging easy laps. Behind the glass partition at the hundred-meter mark, two coaches sat with tablets on their knees, watching telemetry.

The start tone sounded. He drove out of the blocks.

The first fifty meters were the part that still felt like running. His feet hit rubber, knees drove forward, and the initial acceleration built i...
open_hooks_to_carry: Margot plugs into Tomas during hurdle training and pushes his heart rate past the safety margin
Tomas stumbles and falls—minor injury, but the heart-rate spike is flagged in telemetry
Post-feed comedown: the flatness of Margot's own body, her own unplugged senses
prev_tail_excerpt:
...way they did when a word had dropped out of reach. "Cadbury World," she said at last, smiling over the gap she'd just shown him. "She's never been. I've been four times and it's still good. They give you free chocolate at the end."

"Tell Priya I said hi."

"Will do. Eat a vegetable."

"Night, Farah."

"Night, Yu."

The screen went dark. Yusuf sat with the mug cooling in his hands and counted backward through the call: jars, Cadbury World, the half-beat before hopeless. Two words had gone missing this month. Last month there had been one.

There was no treatment. There were only papers full of might and could. The one he kept going back to, fourteen months old now, had a sentence highlighted in yellow: high-resolution neural maps from enhanced systems might trace the degenerative pathway closely enough to target it. Meridian owned most of those maps. Their reply to his access request still sat starred in his inbox with a licensing quote on page two that would've gutted a small lab.

He reopened the laptop. Today's correlation file waited on the desktop. The incident portal loaded its usual boxes: anomaly class, affected athlete, source dataset, retention authorization. He could fill the first two. The third led straight to the hidden directory. The fourth asked why he'd been keeping other people's raw physiology in the drawer beside his tea.

He opened the encrypted drive, dragged the correlation file into the archive, and watched it vanish into the dated folder. Then he closed the incident form blank, ejected the drive, put it back in the drawer, and turned off the light.
secondary_character_beats: Tomas's fall is minor but the data trail exists; his trust in his coaching team is undamaged, which makes the later betrayal worse

setups_to_plant.description: Margot routinely elevates athletes' heart rates beyond safe margins during feed sessions—the pattern is there in the telemetry data for anyone who reviews it

state_deltas_required: Margot's addiction to the sensory feed—the borrowed intensity of someone else's body
Margot's method visibly costs an athlete physically; she conceals the cause
